content.scenario: German A1: Greetings and Farewells 🗣️
content.text: In German, how do you say 'Good morning'? {{c1::Guten Morgen}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Good morning' en German? Guten Morgen
content.usage: Term: Guten Morgen | Meaning: Good morning

content.scenario: German A1: Greetings and Farewells 🗣️
content.text: In German, how do you say 'Good day / Hello'? {{c1::Guten Tag}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Good day / Hello' en German? Guten Tag
content.usage: Term: Guten Tag | Meaning: Good day / Hello

content.scenario: German A1: Greetings and Farewells 🗣️
content.text: In German, how do you say 'Good evening'? {{c1::Guten Abend}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Good evening' en German? Guten Abend
content.usage: Term: Guten Abend | Meaning: Good evening

content.scenario: German A1: Greetings and Farewells 🗣️
content.text: In German, how do you say 'How are you? - Formal'? {{c1::Wie geht es Ihnen?}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'How are you? - Formal' en German? Wie geht es Ihnen?
content.usage: Term: Wie geht es Ihnen? | Meaning: How are you? - Formal

content.scenario: German A1: Greetings and Farewells 🗣️
content.text: In German, how do you say 'Hello'? {{c1::Hallo}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.

content.scenario: German A1: Greetings and Farewells 🗣️
content.text: In German, how do you say 'Goodbye - Formal'? {{c1::Auf Wiedersehen}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Goodbye - Formal' en German? Auf Wiedersehen
content.usage: Term: Auf Wiedersehen | Meaning: Goodbye - Formal

content.scenario: German A1: Greetings and Farewells 🗣️
content.text: In German, how do you say 'Bye - Informal'? {{c1::Tschüss}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Bye - Informal' en German? Tschüss
content.usage: Term: Tschüss | Meaning: Bye - Informal

content.scenario: German A1: Greetings and Farewells 🗣️
content.text: In German, how do you say 'Good night'? {{c1::Gute Nacht}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Good night' en German? Gute Nacht
content.usage: Term: Gute Nacht | Meaning: Good night

content.scenario: German A1: Greetings 🇩🇪
content.text: In a business setting or with strangers, you should greet them formally with: '{{c1::Guten Morgen}}' (until 11 AM) or '{{c1::Guten Tag}}' (11 AM to 6 PM).
content.explanation: German distinguishes strictly between formal and informal greetings. Formal greetings are used with the pronoun 'Sie' and change based on the time of day.
content.spanish: En un ámbito empresarial o con extraños, debes saludarlos formalmente con: 'Guten Morgen' (hasta las 11 AM) o 'Guten Tag' (de 11 AM a 6 PM).
content.usage:
Time-based formal greetings:
  • Guten Morgen (Good morning - until 11 AM)
  • Guten Tag (Good day - 11 AM to 6 PM)
  • Guten Abend (Good evening - after 6 PM)

content.scenario: German A1: Greetings 🇩🇪
content.text: To ask a stranger 'How are you?' formally in German, you say: '{{c1::Wie geht es Ihnen?}}'
content.explanation: Ihnen is the dative case of the formal pronoun 'Sie'. For friends or peers, you would use the informal 'Wie geht es dir?' or simply 'Wie geht's?'.
content.spanish: Para preguntar formalmente a un extraño '¿Cómo está usted?' en alemán, dices: 'Wie geht es Ihnen?'.
content.usage:
Usage examples:
  • Formal: Wie geht es Ihnen, Herr Müller? (How are you, Mr. Müller?)
  • Informal: Wie geht es dir, Anna? (How are you, Anna?)

content.scenario: German A1: Goodbyes 🇩🇪
content.text: A common informal way to say goodbye to friends in German is '{{c1::Tschüss}}' or '{{c1::Bis bald}}' (See you soon).
content.explanation: Tschüss is the most common informal goodbye in Germany. Auf Wiedersehen is formal and used in shops, business meetings, or with strangers.
content.spanish: Una forma informal común de despedirse de los amigos en alemán es 'Tschüss' o 'Bis bald' (Hasta pronto).
content.usage:
Goodbye options:
  • Informal: Tschüss! (Bye!) or Bis später! (See you later!)
  • Formal: Auf Wiedersehen! (Goodbye!)

content.scenario: German A1: Greetings and Farewells 🗣️
content.text: In German, how do you say 'Welcome'? {{c1::Willkommen}}
content.explanation: Used to welcome guests. Often prefixed as 'Herzlich willkommen' (A warm welcome).
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Welcome' en alemán? Willkommen
content.usage: Term: Willkommen | Example: Herzlich willkommen in Berlin! (A warm welcome to Berlin!)

content.scenario: German A1: Greetings and Farewells 🗣️
content.text: In German, how do you say 'See you tomorrow'? {{c1::Bis morgen}}
content.explanation: Literally 'until tomorrow'. Standard parting phrase for classmates, colleagues, or friends.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'See you tomorrow' en alemán? Bis morgen
content.usage: Term: Bis morgen | Example: Schönen Feierabend, bis morgen! (Have a nice evening, see you tomorrow!)

content.scenario: German A1: Greetings and Farewells 🗣️
content.text: In German, how do you say 'See you later'? {{c1::Bis später}}
content.explanation: Literally 'until later'. Used when you expect to see the person again on the same day.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'See you later' en alemán? Bis später
content.usage: Term: Bis später | Example: Ich muss los, bis später! (I have to go, see you later!)

content.scenario: German A1: Greetings and Farewells 🗣️
content.text: In German, the extremely versatile word for both 'Please' and 'You're welcome' is '{{c1::Bitte}}'.
content.explanation: Depending on context, 'Bitte' can mean please, you're welcome, here you go, or even 'pardon?' when you didn't hear something.
content.spanish: En alemán, la palabra 'Bitte' es muy versátil y significa tanto 'Por favor' como 'De nada'.
content.usage: Examples: Ein Bier, bitte. (A beer, please.) | - Danke! - Bitte! (- Thanks! - You're welcome!)

content.scenario: German A1: Greetings and Farewells 🗣️
content.text: In German, how do you say 'Thank you very much'? {{c1::Danke schön}} or {{c1::Vielen Dank}}.
content.explanation: Both are polite ways to express strong gratitude. 'Vielen Dank' literally means 'many thanks'.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Muchas gracias' en alemán? Danke schön / Vielen Dank.
content.usage: Examples: Danke schön für das Geschenk. (Thank you very much for the gift.) | Vielen Dank für Ihre Hilfe. (Many thanks for your help.)

content.scenario: German A1: Greetings and Farewells 🗣️
content.text: In German, a polite and common way to say 'You're welcome / My pleasure' is '{{c1::Gern geschehen}}'.
content.explanation: Literally means 'happily occurred'. Used in response to a thank you.
content.spanish: En alemán, una respuesta cortés a 'gracias' es 'Gern geschehen' (Con gusto / De nada).
content.usage: Dialogue: - Vielen Dank! - Gern geschehen!

content.scenario: German A1: Greetings and Farewells 🗣️
content.text: In German, the formal question to ask someone their name is '{{c1::Wie heißen Sie?}}' and the informal is '{{c2::Wie heißt du?}}'.
content.explanation: Uses the verb 'heißen' (to be named). Note the capital 'S' in formal 'Sie'.
content.spanish: En alemán, la pregunta formal para preguntar el nombre es 'Wie heißen Sie?' y la informal es 'Wie heißt du?'.
content.usage: Formal: Guten Tag, wie heißen Sie? | Informal: Hallo, wie heißt du?

content.scenario: German A1: Greetings and Farewells 🗣️
content.text: In German, how do you say 'My name is [Name]'? {{c1::Ich heiße}} [Name] or {{c1::Mein Name ist}} [Name].
content.explanation: You can use the verb 'heißen' or the phrase 'Mein Name ist...'.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Me llamo...' o 'Mi nombre es...' en alemán? Ich heiße / Mein Name ist.
content.usage: Examples: Ich heiße Max. | Mein Name ist Max.

content.scenario: German A1: Greetings and Farewells 🗣️
content.text: In German, how do you say 'Have a nice day' to wish someone well upon parting? {{c1::Schönen Tag noch}}
content.explanation: Commonly said by shopkeepers, cashiers, or friends when saying goodbye during the day.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Que tenga un buen día' al despedirse en alemán? Schönen Tag noch.
content.usage: Term: Schönen Tag noch! | Dialogue: - Tschüss! - Schönen Tag noch!

content.scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In German, how do you say 'I am'? {{c1::Ich bin}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'I am' en German? Ich bin
content.usage: Term: Ich bin | Meaning: I am

content.scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In German, how do you say 'You are'? {{c1::Du bist}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'You are' en German? Du bist
content.usage: Term: Du bist | Meaning: You are

content.scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In German, how do you say 'He/She/It is'? {{c1::Er/Sie/Es ist}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'He/She/It is' en German? Er/Sie/Es ist
content.usage: Term: Er/Sie/Es ist | Meaning: He/She/It is

content.scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In German, how do you say 'We are'? {{c1::Wir sind}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'We are' en German? Wir sind
content.usage: Term: Wir sind | Meaning: We are

content.scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In German, how do you say 'You are'? {{c1::Ihr seid}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'You are' en German? Ihr seid
content.usage: Term: Ihr seid | Meaning: You are

content.scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In German, how do you say 'You are (formal) / They are'? {{c1::Sie sind}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'You are (formal) / They are' en German? Sie sind
content.usage: Term: Sie sind | Meaning: You are (formal) / They are

content.scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In German, what is the infinitive form of the verb 'to be'? {{c1::sein}}
content.explanation: German verb conjugation of 'sein' is highly irregular and one of the most basic elements of A1 grammar.
content.spanish: ¿Cuál es el infinitivo del verbo 'ser/estar' en alemán? sein
content.usage: Term: sein | Example: glücklich sein (to be happy)

content.scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: To negate the verb 'sein' in German, you place the negation particle {{c1::nicht}} after the verb. Example: I am not ready = {{c2::Ich bin nicht bereit}}.
content.explanation: 'nicht' is used to negate adjectives, adverbs, pronouns, and verbs in German.
content.spanish: Para negar el verbo 'sein' en alemán, se coloca 'nicht' después del verbo conjugado. 'Ich bin nicht' = Yo no soy/estoy.
content.usage: Example: Er ist nicht hier. (He is not here.)

content.scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In German, after accusative prepositions like 'für' (for), the pronoun 'ich' becomes {{c1::mich}} (me) and 'du' becomes {{c2::dich}} (you).
content.explanation: 'für' is a preposition that always demands the accusative case.
content.spanish: En alemán, después de preposiciones acusativas como 'für', 'ich' se convierte en 'mich' y 'du' en 'dich'.
content.usage: Examples: Das ist für mich. (This is for me.) | Ich habe etwas für dich. (I have something for you.)

content.scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In German, after dative prepositions like 'mit' (with), 'er' (he) becomes {{c1::ihm}} (him) and 'sie' (she) becomes {{c2::ihr}} (her).
content.explanation: 'mit' is a preposition that always demands the dative case.
content.spanish: En alemán, después de preposiciones dativas como 'mit', 'er' se convierte en 'ihm' y 'sie' en 'ihr'.
content.usage: Examples: Ich gehe mit ihm. (I am going with him.) | Wir sprechen mit ihr. (We are speaking with her.)

content.scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In German, after dative prepositions like 'mit' (with), 'wir' (we) becomes {{c1::uns}} (us) and 'ihr' (you all) becomes {{c2::euch}} (you all).
content.explanation: Dative forms for first and second-person plural pronouns.
content.spanish: En alemán, después de 'mit', 'wir' se convierte en 'uns' y 'ihr' en 'euch'.
content.usage: Examples: Komm mit uns! (Come with us!) | Ich fahre mit euch. (I am driving with you all.)

content.scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In German, after dative prepositions like 'mit' (with), 'sie' (they) becomes {{c1::ihnen}} (them) and formal 'Sie' (you) becomes {{c2::Ihnen}} (you formal).
content.explanation: Note that 'Ihnen' (formal you) is always capitalized, whereas 'ihnen' (them) is lowercase.
content.spanish: En alemán, después de 'mit', el pronombre 'sie' (ellos/ellas) se convierte en 'ihnen' y 'Sie' (formal) se convierte en 'Ihnen'.
content.usage: Examples: Wie geht es Ihnen? (How are you? - formal) | Ich arbeite mit ihnen. (I work with them.)

content.scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: To form a question in German using the verb 'sein', you swap the subject and verb: 'Are you ready?' is '{{c1::Bist du bereit?}}' and 'Are you sure (formal)?' is '{{c2::Sind Sie sicher?}}'.
content.explanation: German questions are formed by placing the inflected verb at the first position (verb-initial).
content.spanish: Para formar preguntas en alemán con 'sein', se invierte el sujeto y el verbo. 'Bist du bereit?' = ¿Estás listo?
content.usage: Examples: Bist du bereit? (Are you ready?) | Sind Sie sicher? (Are you sure? - formal)

content.scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In German, neuter nouns (like 'das Kind' or 'das Buch') are replaced by the personal pronoun {{c1::es}}. Example: Das Buch ist alt. {{c2::Es}} ist gut. (The book is old. It is good.)
content.explanation: Neuter nouns are referred to by 'es', masculine by 'er', and feminine by 'sie'.
content.spanish: En alemán, los sustantivos neutros se reemplazan por el pronombre 'es'. Por ejemplo, 'das Buch' -> 'es'.
content.usage: Example: Das Buch ist alt. Es ist gut. (The book is old. It is good.)

content.scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In German, what is the gender-neutral demonstrative phrase used to say 'this is / that is / these are / those are'? {{c1::Das ist}} or {{c1::Das sind}}.
content.explanation: 'Das ist' is used for singular subjects, and 'Das sind' is used for plural subjects, regardless of gender.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Esto es / Estos son' en alemán de manera invariable? Das ist (singular) / Das sind (plural).
content.usage: Examples: Das ist mein Freund. (This is my friend.) | Das sind meine Bücher. (These are my books.)

content.scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In German, how do you say 'Who is that?'? {{c1::Wer ist das?}}
content.explanation: Wer is the question word for 'Who' in German.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Quién es ese/a?' en alemán? Wer ist das?
content.usage: Dialogue: - Wer ist das? - Das ist meine Schwester. (- Who is that? - That is my sister.)

content.scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In German, how do you say 'What is that?'? {{c1::Was ist das?}}
content.explanation: Was is the question word for 'What' in German.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Qué es eso?' en alemán? Was ist das?
content.usage: Example: Was ist das dort auf dem Tisch? (What is that there on the table?)

content.scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In German, what is the singular masculine subject pronoun? {{c1::er}}
content.explanation: Used for male persons and any masculine grammatical noun (e.g. 'der Tisch' -> 'er ist').
content.spanish: ¿Cuál es el pronombre de sujeto singular masculino en alemán? er
content.usage: Example: Wo ist der Stuhl? Er ist hier. (Where is the chair? It is here.)

content.scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In German, what is the singular feminine subject pronoun? {{c1::sie}}
content.explanation: Used for female persons and any feminine grammatical noun (e.g. 'die Tasche' -> 'sie ist').
content.spanish: ¿Cuál es el pronombre de sujeto singular femenino en alemán? sie
content.usage: Example: Wo ist die Tasche? Sie ist weg. (Where is the bag? It is gone.)

content.scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In German, the formal 'you' is '{{c1::Sie}}' and the informal plural 'you all' is '{{c2::ihr}}'.
content.explanation: The formal 'Sie' is always capitalized and conjugates like the plural 'they' (sie). The informal 'ihr' is lowercase and has its own conjugation.
content.spanish: En alemán, el 'usted' formal es 'Sie' (siempre en mayúsculas) y el 'vosotros/ustedes' informal es 'ihr'.
content.usage: Formal: Sind Sie müde? | Informal plural: Seid ihr müde?
